{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","old":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Increase capacity with a still-simple wider micro-ResNet and one extra residual block, which should improve fixed-step learning on full CIFAR-10 without introducing optimizer or loop complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The widened/deeper model likely remains undertrained after only 256 fixed steps. Doubling Adam's learning rate is a simple way to improve early optimization without adding architectural or loop complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current configuration is learning well, so the next low-complexity optimizer change is to decouple weight decay from Adam's adaptive updates. AdamW often improves validation loss in short-budget image training without requiring broader loop changes.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"With only 256 training steps and no label noise, the model is more likely underfitting than overfitting. Removing weight decay is a simple way to let the current AdamW-plus-cosine setup fit faster and potentially reduce validation loss further.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Removing weight decay slightly hurt validation loss, so the next disciplined move is to restore the better regularized setting rather than stacking more changes on top of a regression. This reverts to the strongest visible optimizer configuration so far and keeps the search focused.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, the current setup only sees a modest fraction of the training set. Increasing batch size is a simple way to expose the model to more unique examples within the same step budget while leaving the optimizer and architecture otherwise intact.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump from batch 64 to 128 produced the largest gain so far, which suggests this fixed-step regime benefits strongly from seeing more unique training examples. Pushing to batch 256 is the most direct follow-up test while preserving the rest of the now-strong optimizer and scheduler setup.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger batch size is now doing most of the work, and that often supports a higher learning rate without destabilizing AdamW. A moderate increase to 1.5e-3 is a focused test for faster progress within the same 256-step budget while avoiding the larger runtime risk of pushing batch size even higher.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Both larger batches and the previous learning-rate increase produced substantial gains, so the next highest-value test is another moderate LR increase. With AdamW, cosine decay, and batch 256 already stable, 2e-3 is a plausible next step for faster optimization within the fixed training budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Validation loss has kept improving as learning rate increased under the larger batch regime, so the most efficient next search move is another incremental LR step rather than a more disruptive architecture change. Moving from 2e-3 to 2.5e-3 probes whether there is still headroom before optimization becomes unstable.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The search is still on the improving side of the learning-rate curve, but gains are getting smaller, so the next step should stay incremental. Raising LR from 2.5e-3 to 3e-3 tests for a bit more short-budget optimization headroom while keeping the rest of the strong configuration fixed.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current search path is still producing reliable gains from higher learning rates, so the next sensible move is to continue climbing in small steps instead of changing multiple axes at once. Increasing to 3.5e-3 tests whether the short-budget optimum is still above the current setting while keeping the experiment low-complexity and easy to interpret.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gains in this run came from exposing the model to more examples per fixed training step, and batch 256 has remained stable with the current optimizer settings. Moving to batch 384 is a measured follow-up that tests whether there is still useful data-throughput headroom without jumping all the way to a much riskier doubling.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger batch continues to improve the fixed-step regime, which usually increases the stable learning-rate range. Since 3.5e-3 was only slightly too high at batch 256, it is a strong next test at batch 384 and is lower risk than another large runtime jump from pushing batch size even further.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant improvement pattern in this run has been increasing the number of examples processed per fixed training step, and the current batch-384 configuration remains stable while still improving. With only a few trials left, batch 512 is the highest-value throughput test to see whether another jump in sample coverage can outweigh the additional per-step cost.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At batch 512 the optimization regime is even more data-rich and typically tolerates a somewhat larger learning rate. With only a couple of trials left, increasing to 4e-3 is the most direct way to test for additional short-budget optimization headroom without adding more runtime-heavy changes.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last increase overshot the optimum, and with one trial left the highest-value move is to return to the strongest validated learning rate for the batch-512 regime rather than gambling on a new multi-axis change. This restores the best-performing configuration seen at the current throughput level.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}
